ui.bank_tip: Start with the recurrence relation. Then think about base cases, memoization, and tabulation.
ui.editor_tip: Think in terms of states and transitions. Write recurrence first.

questions.title: Fibonacci — Recursion vs Dynamic Programming
questions.prompt:
The Fibonacci sequence is defined as:

F(0) = 0
F(1) = 1
F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2) for n >= 2

Write a function to compute F(n).

This problem is important because it is the simplest example of overlapping subproblems. A plain recursive solution repeats the same work many times. Dynamic Programming avoids that repetition.

You should understand:
1. why naive recursion is slow
2. how memoization stores previously computed answers
3. how tabulation builds the answer from the bottom up

hints: Write the recurrence relation first.
What are the base cases?
Notice that recursive calls repeat the same subproblems.
Use memoization or tabulation to avoid recomputation.
questions.starter_code:
def fib(n):
    # TODO
    return 0

print(fib(0))
print(fib(1))
print(fib(6))
print(fib(8))
questions.solution_code:
def fib(n):
    if n == 0:
        return 0
    if n == 1:
        return 1

    dp = [0] * (n + 1)
    dp[1] = 1

    for i in range(2, n + 1):
        dp[i] = dp[i - 1] + dp[i - 2]

    return dp[n]

print(fib(0))
print(fib(1))
print(fib(6))
print(fib(8))

questions.title: Climbing Stairs
questions.prompt:
You are climbing a staircase with n steps.

Each time, you may climb either:
- 1 step
- 2 steps

Return the number of distinct ways to reach the top.

This is one of the most important beginner DP questions because it teaches how to define a state. If ways[i] means the number of ways to reach step i, then:

ways[i] = ways[i-1] + ways[i-2]

Why?
Because the last move to step i must come either from step i-1 or step i-2.
questions.svg_html: <svg viewBox="0 0 980 320" width="100%" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Climbing stairs dynamic programming"><rect width="980" height="320" rx="24" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.72)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="30" y="42" font-size="24" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Climbing Stairs</text><text x="30" y="74" font-size="16" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">To reach step i, come from i-1 or i-2.</text><rect x="120" y="232" width="120" height="42" rx="10" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.18)"/><text x="180" y="259" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Step 1</text><rect x="280" y="184" width="120" height="42" rx="10" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.18)"/><text x="340" y="211" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Step 2</text><rect x="440" y="136" width="120" height="42" rx="10" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.18)"/><text x="500" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Step 3</text><rect x="600" y="88" width="120" height="42" rx="10" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.30)"/><text x="660" y="115" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Top</text><line x1="240" y1="232" x2="280" y2="205" stroke="#1f2328" stroke-width="4"/><line x1="240" y1="232" x2="440" y2="157" stroke="#1f2328" stroke-width="4"/><line x1="400" y1="184" x2="440" y2="157" stroke="#1f2328" stroke-width="4"/><line x1="400" y1="184" x2="600" y2="109" stroke="#1f2328" stroke-width="4"/><line x1="560" y1="136" x2="600" y2="109" stroke="#1f2328" stroke-width="4"/><text x="30" y="298" font-size="16" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">ways[i] = ways[i-1] + ways[i-2]</text></svg>
hints: Define dp[i] as the number of ways to reach step i.
What are dp[1] and dp[2]?
Every final move is either a 1-step move or a 2-step move.
So dp[i] depends on the previous two states.
questions.starter_code:
def climb_stairs(n):
    # TODO
    return 0

print(climb_stairs(1))
print(climb_stairs(2))
print(climb_stairs(3))
print(climb_stairs(5))
questions.solution_code:
def climb_stairs(n):
    if n <= 2:
        return n

    dp = [0] * (n + 1)
    dp[1] = 1
    dp[2] = 2

    for i in range(3, n + 1):
        dp[i] = dp[i - 1] + dp[i - 2]

    return dp[n]

print(climb_stairs(1))
print(climb_stairs(2))
print(climb_stairs(3))
print(climb_stairs(5))

questions.title: Minimum Cost Climbing Stairs
questions.prompt:
You are given an array cost where cost[i] is the cost of stepping on stair i.

Once you pay the cost, you may climb either 1 or 2 steps.
You may start from step 0 or step 1.

Return the minimum cost to reach the top.

This problem is important because now the state is not counting ways. Instead, the state represents the minimum cost needed to reach each position.

Transition idea:
To reach step i, you can come from i-1 or i-2.
So choose the cheaper of those two paths.
questions.svg_html: <svg viewBox="0 0 1020 340" width="100%" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Minimum cost climbing stairs"><rect width="1020" height="340" rx="24" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.72)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="30" y="42" font-size="24" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Minimum Cost Climbing Stairs</text><text x="30" y="74" font-size="16" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">At each step, choose the cheaper path from the previous two steps.</text><rect x="110" y="236" width="120" height="48" rx="12" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.18)"/><text x="170" y="267" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">10</text><rect x="280" y="184" width="120" height="48" rx="12" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.26)"/><text x="340" y="215" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">15</text><rect x="450" y="132" width="120" height="48" rx="12" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.18)"/><text x="510" y="163" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">20</text><rect x="640" y="82" width="120" height="48" rx="12" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.30)"/><text x="700" y="113" text-anchor="middle" font-size="18" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Top</text><line x1="230" y1="236" x2="280" y2="208" stroke="#1f2328" stroke-width="4"/><line x1="230" y1="236" x2="450" y2="156" stroke="#1f2328" stroke-width="4"/><line x1="400" y1="184" x2="450" y2="156" stroke="#1f2328" stroke-width="4"/><line x1="400" y1="184" x2="640" y2="106" stroke="#1f2328" stroke-width="4"/><line x1="570" y1="132" x2="640" y2="106" stroke="#1f2328" stroke-width="4"/><text x="30" y="312" font-size="16" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2])</text></svg>
hints: This time the state is minimum cost, not number of ways.
Define dp[i] as the minimum cost to reach step i.
You can come from one step before or two steps before.
Take the smaller of those two previous costs.
questions.starter_code:
def min_cost_climbing_stairs(cost):
    # TODO
    return 0

print(min_cost_climbing_stairs([10, 15, 20]))
print(min_cost_climbing_stairs([1, 100, 1, 1, 1, 100, 1, 1, 100, 1]))
questions.solution_code:
def min_cost_climbing_stairs(cost):
    n = len(cost)
    dp = [0] * (n + 1)

    for i in range(2, n + 1):
        one_step = dp[i - 1] + cost[i - 1]
        two_steps = dp[i - 2] + cost[i - 2]
        dp[i] = min(one_step, two_steps)

    return dp[n]

print(min_cost_climbing_stairs([10, 15, 20]))
print(min_cost_climbing_stairs([1, 100, 1, 1, 1, 100, 1, 1, 100, 1]))

questions.title: House Robber
questions.prompt:
You are a robber planning to rob houses along a street.
Each house has some amount of money.

Constraint:
You cannot rob two adjacent houses.

Return the maximum amount of money you can rob.

This is a classic hiring question because it teaches choice-based DP.
For each house, you have two options:
1. rob it → then you must skip the previous house
2. skip it → then keep the best answer up to the previous house

So the recurrence becomes:

dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i])

hints: At each house, you either rob it or skip it.
If you rob it, you cannot rob the previous one.
If you skip it, the answer stays the same as the previous house.
Take the better of the two choices.
questions.starter_code:

    # TODO

print(rob([1, 2, 3, 1]))
print(rob([2, 7, 9, 3, 1]))

questions.title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
questions.prompt:
Given an integer array nums, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence.

A subsequence does not need to be contiguous.

This is a major interview problem because it teaches 2-level state reasoning.
Define dp[i] as:

The length of the longest increasing subsequence ending at index i.

Then for each i, look at all previous indices j where nums[j] < nums[i].
If nums[i] can extend the subsequence ending at j, then:

dp[i] = max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1)

This is one of the most important classic DP problems.
questions.svg_html: <svg viewBox="0 0 1080 360" width="100%" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Longest increasing subsequence"><rect width="1080" height="360" rx="24" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.72)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.18)"/><text x="30" y="42" font-size="24" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">Longest Increasing Subsequence</text><text x="30" y="74" font-size="16" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">dp[i] = best increasing subsequence length ending at i</text><g font-size="22" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328"><rect x="56" y="156" width="84" height="60" rx="12" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.96)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.15)"/><text x="98" y="195" text-anchor="middle">10</text><rect x="154" y="156" width="84" height="60" rx="12" fill="rgba(255,255,255,0.96)" stroke="rgba(31,35,40,0.15)"/><text x="196" y="195" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="252" y="156" width="84" height="60" rx="12" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.18)"/><text x="294" y="195" text-anchor="middle">2</text><rect x="350" y="156" width="84" height="60" rx="12" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.18)"/><text x="392" y="195" text-anchor="middle">5</text><rect x="448" y="156" width="84" height="60" rx="12" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.18)"/><text x="490" y="195" text-anchor="middle">3</text><rect x="546" y="156" width="84" height="60" rx="12" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.18)"/><text x="588" y="195" text-anchor="middle">7</text><rect x="644" y="156" width="108" height="60" rx="12" fill="rgba(255,159,28,0.30)"/><text x="698" y="195" text-anchor="middle">101</text></g><line x1="294" y1="146" x2="392" y2="122" stroke="#1f2328" stroke-width="4"/><line x1="392" y1="122" x2="588" y2="98" stroke="#1f2328" stroke-width="4"/><line x1="588" y1="98" x2="698" y2="74" stroke="#1f2328" stroke-width="4"/><text x="520" y="288" text-anchor="middle" font-size="20" font-weight="900" fill="#1f2328">One LIS: 2 → 5 → 7 → 101</text><text x="30" y="324" font-size="16" font-weight="700" fill="#5b6570">Subsequence is not the same as subarray. It does not need to be contiguous.</text></svg>
hints: Define dp[i] carefully.
Every element can start a subsequence of length 1.
For each i, check all j < i.
If nums[j] < nums[i], then nums[i] can extend that subsequence.
questions.starter_code:
def length_of_lis(nums):
    # TODO
    return 0

print(length_of_lis([10, 9, 2, 5, 3, 7, 101, 18]))
print(length_of_lis([0, 1, 0, 3, 2, 3]))
print(length_of_lis([7, 7, 7, 7, 7]))
